structure_blocks.heading: Contract amendment
structure_blocks.text: Contract amendment

structure_blocks.heading: Contract amendment
structure_blocks.text: The contract amendment approval document is used to seek approval to amend a contract, standing offer (SO) or supply arrangement (SA). The amendment approval authority required will determine the document to be used.

structure_blocks.heading: Contract amendment
structure_blocks.text: Here are some general considerations to keep in mind when preparing a contract amendment:

structure_blocks.heading: Contract amendment
structure_blocks.text: Evaluate the current state of the contract, including progress and any previous amendments

structure_blocks.heading: Contract amendment
structure_blocks.text: Outline the details of the proposed amendment

structure_blocks.heading: Contract amendment
structure_blocks.text: Provide a clear rationale for any changes in the payment basis, methods, or overall contract terms, supported by detailed documentation

structure_blocks.heading: Contract amendment
structure_blocks.text: Consult Legal Services before considering a conditional amendment

structure_blocks.heading: Contract amendment
structure_blocks.text: There are four types of contract amendments. The following outlines the four types and the approval authority required for each.

structure_blocks.heading: 1. Administrative contract amendments
structure_blocks.text: 1. Administrative contract amendments

structure_blocks.heading: 1. Administrative contract amendments
structure_blocks.text: Administrative contract amendments are used for changes that do not create additional risk or liability to Canada and do not increase the contract value (that is, a nil or reduction in contract value amendment) They typically include, changes to update contact information or reduce the contract value for quantities of goods or work no longer required under the contract, with no increase in the unit price.

structure_blocks.heading: 1. Administrative contract amendments
structure_blocks.text: Since there is no impact on the approval value and no additional risk or liability to Canada they do not require a Risk Assessment for Amendment (RAA).

structure_blocks.heading: 1. Administrative contract amendments
structure_blocks.text: Approval authority: An administrative contract amendment can be approved and signed by any incumbent, under any level, who holds a valid delegation of approval authority for contract entry for the applicable product and service categories

structure_blocks.heading: 2. Nil or reduction in contract value amendments involving additional risk to Canada
heading_path: 2. Nil or reduction in contract value amendments involving additional risk to Canada
structure_blocks.text: 2. Nil or reduction in contract value amendments involving additional risk to Canada

structure_blocks.heading: 2. Nil or reduction in contract value amendments involving additional risk to Canada
heading_path: 2. Nil or reduction in contract value amendments involving additional risk to Canada
structure_blocks.text: A contract amendment can involve additional risks to Canada even though the contract value is not changing or is being reduced. This applies to amendments that propose changes to the basis of payment, the method of payment, or the contract conditions, so that additional risk or liability is transferred to Canada.

structure_blocks.heading: 2. Nil or reduction in contract value amendments involving additional risk to Canada
heading_path: 2. Nil or reduction in contract value amendments involving additional risk to Canada
structure_blocks.text: This type of amendment can be used when a client:

structure_blocks.heading: 2. Nil or reduction in contract value amendments involving additional risk to Canada
heading_path: 2. Nil or reduction in contract value amendments involving additional risk to Canada
structure_blocks.text: reduces the quantity required but the contractor wants to increase the price because of the reduced quantity, triggering a need for renegotiation

structure_blocks.heading: 2. Nil or reduction in contract value amendments involving additional risk to Canada
heading_path: 2. Nil or reduction in contract value amendments involving additional risk to Canada
structure_blocks.text: requires a work package to be removed from a research and development contract, but there is no clear relationship between the reduced cost and the reduced work package

structure_blocks.heading: 2. Nil or reduction in contract value amendments involving additional risk to Canada
heading_path: 2. Nil or reduction in contract value amendments involving additional risk to Canada
structure_blocks.text: requests you to amend a contract by deleting or substituting an item or work package

structure_blocks.heading: 2. Nil or reduction in contract value amendments involving additional risk to Canada
heading_path: 2. Nil or reduction in contract value amendments involving additional risk to Canada
structure_blocks.text: Nil or reduction in contract value amendments involving additional risk to Canada require a Risk Assessment for Amendment (RAA) to assess and manage the additional risks involved and must be authorized in accordance with the approval level determined by completing the RAA .

structure_blocks.heading: 3. Pre-approved contract amendments
structure_blocks.text: 3. Pre-approved contract amendments

structure_blocks.heading: 3. Pre-approved contract amendments
structure_blocks.text: Pre-approved contract amendments exercise options or authorize work that was previously identified and approved in the original approval documentation. Pre-approved contract amendments include:

structure_blocks.heading: 3. Pre-approved contract amendments
structure_blocks.text: amendments to exercise an option for additional quantities or years initially included in the original solicitation and approved in the original contract approval submission

structure_blocks.heading: 3. Pre-approved contract amendments
structure_blocks.text: amendments which use amounts set-aside for anticipated but not yet clearly defined work, or changes, such as unscheduled work, design changes, or work arising, that were approved as part of the original contract submission

structure_blocks.heading: 3. Pre-approved contract amendments
structure_blocks.text: This amendment is pre-approved, as the work and funding were identified and approved in the original contract approval documentation. In accordance with departmental guidance, an RAA was completed, with the ''value'' question assessed using the approved set-aside amount. No additional approval beyond the delegated authority identified through the RAA is required.

structure_blocks.heading: 4. Unforeseen contract amendments not pre-approved
structure_blocks.text: 4. Unforeseen contract amendments not pre-approved

structure_blocks.heading: 4. Unforeseen contract amendments not pre-approved
structure_blocks.text: Sometimes contract amendments are required to incorporate work or contract changes that were neither foreseen nor pre-approved in the original contract approval. Unforeseen contract amendments not approved can include:

structure_blocks.heading: 4. Unforeseen contract amendments not pre-approved
structure_blocks.text: purchasing additional quantities not previously anticipated and not pre-approved

structure_blocks.heading: 4. Unforeseen contract amendments not pre-approved
structure_blocks.text: adding an additional requirement to the contract not previously anticipated and not pre-approved

structure_blocks.heading: 4. Unforeseen contract amendments not pre-approved
structure_blocks.text: extending the contract period due to unexpected delays (beyond the contractor’s control) to allow all work under the contract to be completed

structure_blocks.heading: 4. Unforeseen contract amendments not pre-approved
structure_blocks.text: increasing the contract value beyond the original approval

structure_blocks.heading: 4. Unforeseen contract amendments not pre-approved
structure_blocks.text: Approval for this type of amendment must be secured at the appropriate level:

structure_blocks.heading: 4. Unforeseen contract amendments not pre-approved
structure_blocks.text: For amendments within complexity levels 1 through 3 inclusive , the appropriate level required is determined by completing a RAA

structure_blocks.heading: 4. Unforeseen contract amendments not pre-approved
structure_blocks.text: For amendments that fall within complexity levels 4 and 5 , the approval of the Public Services and Procurement Canada (PSPC) Assistant Deputy Minister (ADM), Procurement Branch, or Defence and Marine Procurement Branch, is required

structure_blocks.heading: Preparing and securing approval for contract amendments
heading_path: Preparing and securing approval for contract amendments
structure_blocks.text: When preparing and securing approval for contract amendments, it's essential to consider the nature and value of the proposed changes. This process ensures that all amendments align with organizational policies and receive the necessary approvals.

structure_blocks.heading: Preparing and securing approval for contract amendments
heading_path: Preparing and securing approval for contract amendments
structure_blocks.text: Here's how to determine the required approval for a contract amendment:

structure_blocks.heading: Preparing and securing approval for contract amendments
heading_path: Preparing and securing approval for contract amendments
structure_blocks.text: Before proceeding with a contract amendment, complete the RAA to identify the risks associated with the proposed changes and determine the appropriate approval authority. Note: that administrative amendments and pre-approved options are exceptions to this step.

structure_blocks.heading: Preparing and securing approval for contract amendments
heading_path: Preparing and securing approval for contract amendments
structure_blocks.text: For proposed contract amendments that have not been previously approved and fall within complexity levels 1 through 3, you must complete the RAA to identify the suitable approval authority.

structure_blocks.heading: Preparing and securing approval for contract amendments
heading_path: Preparing and securing approval for contract amendments
structure_blocks.text: When responding to the "value" question in the RAA , consider the total value of the proposed amendment and the cumulative value of all positive amendments (previous and proposed). Therefore, use the aggregate amendment value (both past and proposed) when determining the dollar amount. Note : This excludes pre-approved amendments identified and approved in the original approval documents

structure_blocks.heading: Preparing and securing approval for contract amendments
heading_path: Preparing and securing approval for contract amendments
structure_blocks.text: Unless specified within the notes to contracting limits including exceptional authorities outlined in the PSPC Delegation of authorities instrument (accessible only on the Government of Canada network - for PSPC only), exceptional authorities include both contract entry and amendment authority. Consequently, the dollar value considered must include the original contract value and the aggregate amendment value (both past and proposed).

structure_blocks.heading: Preparing and securing approval for contract amendments
heading_path: Preparing and securing approval for contract amendments
structure_blocks.text: Examples for when a RAA is required for a complexity level 1 through 3 contract awarded on an electronically competed basis can be found on the Determining the Amendment Approval Authority (accessible only on the Government of Canada network).

structure_blocks.heading: Contract amendment scenarios
structure_blocks.text: Contract amendment scenarios

structure_blocks.heading: Contract amendment scenarios
structure_blocks.text: The following are examples of contract amendment scenarios that require detailed attention and adherence to specific procedures.

structure_blocks.heading: Reduction in contract value
structure_blocks.text: Reduction in contract value

structure_blocks.heading: Reduction in contract value
structure_blocks.text: When a client requests fewer quantities without changing the unit price, the designated contracting authority may approve this reduction as an administrative amendment.

structure_blocks.heading: Reduction in contract value
structure_blocks.text: If a quantity reduction leads to a price increase request from the supplier, renegotiation is essential. The approval level for the contract amendment is that required for the revised unit price multiplied by the new quantity. Combined amendment approval authorities apply, and a RAA is required to determine approval level.

structure_blocks.heading: Reduction in contract value
structure_blocks.text: No RAA is required if the client wishes to disencumber funds allocated to a repair and overhaul contract because there will be no more work arising that fiscal year. The contracting authority can approve a contract value reduction as an administrative amendment.

structure_blocks.heading: Reduction in contract value
structure_blocks.text: A RAA is required to determine approval level if a client needs to remove a work package from a research and development contract without a direct relationship between the reduced cost and the reduced work package. The type of amendment is considered to be a reduction in contract value amendment involving additional risk to Canada. The amendment will be approved at a level equal to the value of the proposed cost reduction. Aggregate amendment approval authorities apply. A nil value amendment is not appropriate as there is no link between the value of the work package and the reduction.

structure_blocks.heading: Substitution of an item or work package
heading_path: Substitution of an item or work package
structure_blocks.text: Substitution of an item or work package

structure_blocks.heading: Substitution of an item or work package
heading_path: Substitution of an item or work package
structure_blocks.text: When substituting an item or work package, the value of the new item determines the amendment’s approval level. A RAA must be performed to determine the appropriate approval authority for adding the substitute item or work package.

heading_path: Introduction of additional risks or liability
structure_blocks.text: Amendments that increase risk or liability to Canada are subject to strict review, even if no additional funding is required. Some examples of amendments the introduce additional risk are

structure_blocks.text: extending the period of a contract, contract with task authorizations (TAs), SO , or SA with a specific end date due to contractor delivery date slippage

structure_blocks.heading: Advance approval for options
structure_blocks.text: When you're exercising contract options, follow these specific steps to ensure everything is done correctly:

structure_blocks.heading: Advance approval for options
structure_blocks.text: Get approval for the option: Make sure you have the necessary approval to exercise the contract option. This approval should align with what was outlined in the original approval document. Ensure that all conditions and requirements are met as per the initial contract. Once approval has been obtained to exercise an option, the contract amendment to exercise the option requires only the signature of an incumbent, under any level, who holds a valid delegation of approval authority for contract entry for the same categories for which the incumbent has delegation of authority

structure_blocks.heading: Advance approval for options
structure_blocks.text: Check delegation of approval authority for contract entry : Verify that you have the appropriate delegation of authority to sign the contract amendment. You need to have the authority for the same categories as the contract option. For example, if you have the delegated authority for goods and military repair and overhaul, you can sign for those categories

structure_blocks.heading: Advance approval for options
structure_blocks.text: Separate aggregate values: Treat the aggregate values of previously approved options separately from the aggregate amendment values of either set-asides or normal amendments. You can exercise options up to their maximum value in one amendment if needed

structure_blocks.heading: Advance approval for options
structure_blocks.text: Use funds as approved: Use the funds and approval for the option strictly for the purposes specified in the original approval document. For example, if a one-year services contract is approved for $200,000 with an option year also valued at $200,000, you can't use the option year's funds to cover an increase in services during the first year

structure_blocks.heading: Advance approval for options
structure_blocks.text: Sign the contract amendment: Once you have the necessary approvals and verified your authority, sign the contract amendment. Only someone with the correct delegation of authority for the relevant contract categories should sign

structure_blocks.text: Interpretation of aggregate amendment value

structure_blocks.heading: Interpretation of aggregate amendment value
structure_blocks.text: Aggregate amendment value means the sum total of the amendments to a contract, including positive and negative amendments. It applies only to amendments requiring TB approval.

structure_blocks.heading: Interpretation of aggregate amendment value
structure_blocks.text: For internal purposes and to ensure adequate control measures are in place, the aggregate amount for amendments not pre-approved will be calculated using only positive amendments.

structure_blocks.heading: Interpretation of aggregate amendment value
structure_blocks.text: Nil value amendments where no further risk or liability accrued to Canada are administrative in nature and can be approved by the Contracting Authority. For nil value amendments where further risk or liability will be Canada's responsibility, the completion of a RAA is required to determine the appropriate approval authority.

structure_blocks.heading: Interpretation of aggregate amendment value
structure_blocks.text: Extending the period of a method of supply (that is, a contract, contract with TA s, SO , or SA ) with a specific end date cannot be treated as administrative in nature. Adding additional time to the contract represents additional liability for Canada regardless of whether or not additional funding is required.

structure_blocks.heading: Interpretation of aggregate amendment value
structure_blocks.text: For example, if a contract has been awarded for a two-year period ending March 31 in the amount of $10 million, and there is $4 million left over on April 1st, that $4 million cannot be used for an extension of the contract without obtaining the appropriate approval, even though the original approval to contract was for $10 million.

raw_text:
Part 1 Submission Data – Form PWGSC-TPSGC 1151-1 Proposal Additional costs (or reduction in cost) Remarks Authority Part 2 Supporting Data - Form PWGSC-TPSGC 1151-4 Section A - Physical Progress to Date Section B - Authorities for and Status of Contract, plus Amount of Proposed Amendment Section C - Basis and method of payment as last amended Section D - Basis of recommendation

Contract amendment

The contract amendment approval document is used to seek approval to amend a contract, standing offer (SO) or supply arrangement (SA). The amendment approval authority required will determine the document to be used.

Here are some general considerations to keep in mind when preparing a contract amendment:

Evaluate the current state of the contract, including progress and any previous amendments

Outline the details of the proposed amendment

Provide a clear rationale for any changes in the payment basis, methods, or overall contract terms, supported by detailed documentation

Consult Legal Services before considering a conditional amendment

There are four types of contract amendments. The following outlines the four types and the approval authority required for each.

1. Administrative contract amendments

Administrative contract amendments are used for changes that do not create additional risk or liability to Canada and do not increase the contract value (that is, a nil or reduction in contract value amendment) They typically include, changes to update contact information or reduce the contract value for quantities of goods or work no longer required under the contract, with no increase in the unit price.

Since there is no impact on the approval value and no additional risk or liability to Canada they do not require a Risk Assessment for Amendment (RAA).

Approval authority: An administrative contract amendment can be approved and signed by any incumbent, under any level, who holds a valid delegation of approval authority for contract entry for the applicable product and service categories

2. Nil or reduction in contract value amendments involving additional risk to Canada

A contract amendment can involve additional risks to Canada even though the contract value is not changing or is being reduced. This applies to amendments that propose changes to the basis of payment, the method of payment, or the contract conditions, so that additional risk or liability is transferred to Canada.

This type of amendment can be used when a client:

reduces the quantity required but the contractor wants to increase the price because of the reduced quantity, triggering a need for renegotiation

requires a work package to be removed from a research and development contract, but there is no clear relationship between the reduced cost and the reduced work package

requests you to amend a contract by deleting or substituting an item or work package

Nil or reduction in contract value amendments involving additional risk to Canada require a Risk Assessment for Amendment (RAA) to assess and manage the additional risks involved and must be authorized in accordance with the approval level determined by completing the RAA .

3. Pre-approved contract amendments

Pre-approved contract amendments exercise options or authorize work that was previously identified and approved in the original approval documentation. Pre-approved contract amendments include:

amendments to exercise an option for additional quantities or years initially included in the original solicitation and approved in the original contract approval submission

amendments which use amounts set-aside for anticipated but not yet clearly defined work, or changes, such as unscheduled work, design changes, or work arising, that were approved as part of the original contract submission

This amendment is pre-approved, as the work and funding were identified and approved in the original contract approval documentation. In accordance with departmental guidance, an RAA was completed, with the ''value'' question assessed using the approved set-aside amount. No additional approval beyond the delegated authority identified through the RAA is required.

4. Unforeseen contract amendments not pre-approved

Sometimes contract amendments are required to incorporate work or contract changes that were neither foreseen nor pre-approved in the original contract approval. Unforeseen contract amendments not approved can include:

purchasing additional quantities not previously anticipated and not pre-approved

adding an additional requirement to the contract not previously anticipated and not pre-approved

extending the contract period due to unexpected delays (beyond the contractor’s control) to allow all work under the contract to be completed

increasing the contract value beyond the original approval

Approval for this type of amendment must be secured at the appropriate level:

For amendments within complexity levels 1 through 3 inclusive , the appropriate level required is determined by completing a RAA

For amendments that fall within complexity levels 4 and 5 , the approval of the Public Services and Procurement Canada (PSPC) Assistant Deputy Minister (ADM), Procurement Branch, or Defence and Marine Procurement Branch, is required

Preparing and securing approval for contract amendments

When preparing and securing approval for contract amendments, it's essential to consider the nature and value of the proposed changes. This process ensures that all amendments align with organizational policies and receive the necessary approvals.

Here's how to determine the required approval for a contract amendment:

Before proceeding with a contract amendment, complete the RAA to identify the risks associated with the proposed changes and determine the appropriate approval authority. Note: that administrative amendments and pre-approved options are exceptions to this step.

For proposed contract amendments that have not been previously approved and fall within complexity levels 1 through 3, you must complete the RAA to identify the suitable approval authority.

When responding to the "value" question in the RAA , consider the total value of the proposed amendment and the cumulative value of all positive amendments (previous and proposed). Therefore, use the aggregate amendment value (both past and proposed) when determining the dollar amount. Note : This excludes pre-approved amendments identified and approved in the original approval documents

Unless specified within the notes to contracting limits including exceptional authorities outlined in the PSPC Delegation of authorities instrument (accessible only on the Government of Canada network - for PSPC only), exceptional authorities include both contract entry and amendment authority. Consequently, the dollar value considered must include the original contract value and the aggregate amendment value (both past and proposed).

Examples for when a RAA is required for a complexity level 1 through 3 contract awarded on an electronically competed basis can be found on the Determining the Amendment Approval Authority (accessible only on the Government of Canada network).

Contract amendments requiring Director General or Regional Director General approval and below

You may use form 1151 - Contract Amendment Request (CAR) .

The form is to ensure that all necessary documentation is systematically presented for assessment and approval.

If a RAA is required for the proposed amendment, it must be attached and the results discussed in the contract amendment request.

You must include the following information in the contract amendment request:

a concise description of the goods or services as provided in the original Contract Planning and Advance Approval (CPAA), followed by a clear statement of the proposed amendment’s purpose

total cost of the proposed amendment (increase/decrease) in Canadian dollars or foreign currency, as applicable (if using foreign currency, give the equivalent in Canadian currency for the amendment only)

name of the client and any other relevant information

a table summarizing the approval documents, the document value, the approval value of each approval document, and the approval level

the proposed basis of payment, and justification and support for any proposed changes to the basis of payment

a list of the identified risks, particularly those classified as medium-high to high, and describe the proposed risk response measures for mitigation

provide the following information, as a minimum:

a justification for the amendment

any additional information required for a proper assessment of the proposed amendment (for example, if the amendment is for a substantial cost increase, explain why this additional requirement did not form part of the original requirement)

Amendments requiring approval of Assistant Deputy Minister level and above

Present a comprehensive proposal that includes the amendment's purpose, detailed description of changes, cost implications, and any deviations from standard policies. Outline the methodology for additional costs, including a detailed breakdown and justification for any increases or reductions in the contract value.

You must use the two-part contract amendment request form and present it in either French or English:

PWGSC-TPSGC 1151-1: Contract Request/Contract Amendment Request (Part 1 - Submission Data) (accessible only on the Government of Canada network - for PSPC only), and

PWGSC-TPSGC 1151-4: Contract Amendment Request (Part 2 - Supporting Data) (accessible only on the Government of Canada network - for PSPC only)

Authority to amend contract or authority to revise a standing offer method of supply. Form PWGSC-TPSGC 1151-1 can be used to obtain approval to utilize a standing offer that has been revised by the offeror.

The key elements you should include in a contract amendment request are the purpose of the amendment and the amendment cost. Do not show amounts in Part 1.

The following information provides additional considerations for part 1 of the form, when applicable.

State the purpose of the proposed amendment and briefly describe the goods and/or services, as provided in the original contract request (for example, to amend the contract with ABC for the supply of 20 additional widgets). Include, in the case of goods or services being added, the prices, sales tax position, delivery points, etc.

Identify any differences between funds previously authorized and contract commitments.

If a large number of items are involved, state:

"Unit (and/or lot) prices, totalling $ [insert amount], sales tax [insert sales tax], and FOB [insert FOB], as detailed in the attached annex or in an annex attached to the proposed contract."

If the proposed amendment involves any deviations from Cabinet or TB contracting policies, not included in the original approval, describe the deviations fully.

Show the total cost of the proposed amendment in Canadian dollars or foreign currency, as applicable (if using foreign currency, give the equivalent in Canadian currency).

Show the proposed amended estimated cost of the contract . If the previously authorized total contains an amount for specific future work or foreseen yet unscheduled work (such as design changes or work arising), always include this amount in the total estimated cost. If not, authority for the amount set aside is lost.

Provide the cash flow and other information, as explained under the "Cost" section of the contract request.

Provide a brief description of previous amendments and their cost.

Give additional important information required for a proper assessment of the proposed amendment. For example, if the proposed amendment is for a substantial increase, state why this additional requirement did not form part of the original requirement. Refer to the Remarks section of the Contract Request for a guide to the information which should be provided, if applicable.

When a contract amendment request requires a higher approval level than originally authorized in the contract, detail the basis for the selection of the contractor and the basis of payment. It is not necessary to repeat in Part 1, the present basis of payment if it was previously approved at a higher level or by the contracting authority being approached now for approval of this amendment.

If any alteration in the basis of payment is proposed, provide justification and support. If a difference exists between funds authorized and contractual commitments, explain why.

Provide details surrounding the medium-high and high risks relating to the proposed amendment with proposed mitigation strategies.

If items are being added, or when establishing a firm basis of payment for a contract, previously issued on a price-to-be-negotiated (PTBN) basis, provide price support.

Show the original authority for entry into the contract and the authority for each approved amendment.

When TB authority has been obtained, give the TB number and date. When the Minister's approval has been obtained, state "Ministerial authority." In all other cases, state "departmental authority."

Provide all supporting information, using the following guidance for completion of part 2 of the form.

Summarize the progress of the contract, such as quantities already delivered and the percentage completed; work in progress or completed; advance or progress payments made or any other preliminary expenses; and other matters of a similar nature.

Give specific authorities and authorized amounts under the "Authority and Amount" column for the contract and each amendment (for example, Treasury Board, Minister, Deputy Minister, Director General, Director).

Any differences between authorities (approvals) and commitments should be reconciled in Section B.

If the amount of the proposed amendment exceeds the funds available, it should be noted in this section.

Describe briefly the basis of payment and method of payment as last amended, including sales tax position, delivery terms (for example, FOB ), advance and progress payments).

However, if it is proposed to amend the basis of payment or method of payment, give a detailed description of the present basis of payment and method of payment for any portion of the work for which a new basis of payment or method of payment is being recommended.

Support price and changes in terms or method of payment.

Detail all price support for any items being added or when establishing a firm basis of payment for a contract, previously issued on a PTBN basis.

Explain any discrepancies between the amount approved for the contract and amendments (if any), and the total committed.

Note: If numerous items and unit prices have not been detailed in part 1 of form PWGSC-TPSGC 1151-1 (accessible only on the government of Canada network - for PSPC only), or in an annex to Part 1, make reference in Section D to the specific document of the file that details the information.

Contract amendments requiring Treasury Board approval

For contract amendments that fall under TB authority, a detailed submission is required.

You must use the TB submission form and include:

a clear demonstration of the need for the amendment, its impact on the contract value, and how it adheres to or diverges from TB 's guidelines

consideration of cumulative values of all contract amendments to assess the overall impact on the contract and adherence to exceptional authorities as applicable

For more information refer to the Guidance for Drafters of Treasury Board Submissions .

Authority to amend contract or authority to revise a standing offer method of supply

The form PWGSC-TPSGC 1151-1, Contract Request/Contract Amendment Request (Part 1 – Submission Data) (accessible only on the Government of Canada network - for PSPC only), can be used to obtain approval to utilize an SO that has been revised by the offeror.

Contract amendment scenarios

The following are examples of contract amendment scenarios that require detailed attention and adherence to specific procedures.

Reduction in contract value

When a client requests fewer quantities without changing the unit price, the designated contracting authority may approve this reduction as an administrative amendment.

If a quantity reduction leads to a price increase request from the supplier, renegotiation is essential. The approval level for the contract amendment is that required for the revised unit price multiplied by the new quantity. Combined amendment approval authorities apply, and a RAA is required to determine approval level.

No RAA is required if the client wishes to disencumber funds allocated to a repair and overhaul contract because there will be no more work arising that fiscal year. The contracting authority can approve a contract value reduction as an administrative amendment.

A RAA is required to determine approval level if a client needs to remove a work package from a research and development contract without a direct relationship between the reduced cost and the reduced work package. The type of amendment is considered to be a reduction in contract value amendment involving additional risk to Canada. The amendment will be approved at a level equal to the value of the proposed cost reduction. Aggregate amendment approval authorities apply. A nil value amendment is not appropriate as there is no link between the value of the work package and the reduction.

Substitution of an item or work package

When substituting an item or work package, the value of the new item determines the amendment’s approval level. A RAA must be performed to determine the appropriate approval authority for adding the substitute item or work package.

Introduction of additional risks or liability

Amendments that increase risk or liability to Canada are subject to strict review, even if no additional funding is required. Some examples of amendments the introduce additional risk are

liberalizing progress payments

eliminating performance bond requirements

extending the period of a contract, contract with task authorizations (TAs), SO , or SA with a specific end date due to contractor delivery date slippage

Changes leading to increased risk require authorization aligned with the RAA . In cases lacking corresponding benefits, only TB can authorize extra payments.

Advance approval for amount set aside

Pre-approved funds that are set aside for unforeseen requirements can include:

Utilization of set-aside amounts : If the original authority to enter into a contract also included advance approval for an amount to be set aside for unscheduled work, design changes or work arising, then any amount set aside must be approved by the appropriate non-competitive contract entry approval authority, not to exceed the Director level. The RAA determines the appropriate approval authority. The buyer is to use "set aside amount" as the RAA "value" amount.

Exceeding set-aside amounts : If an amendment to use the remainder of the amount set aside for a specific purpose exceeds the amount set aside, the approval authority for the excess amount will revert back to the appropriate aggregate amendment approval authority in accordance with the Delegation of authorities instrument . Aggregate amendment values of set asides, previously approved, are separate from the aggregate amendment values of either options or normal amendments. if required, set-asides can be exercised to the maximum value in one amendment

Important information for advance approval for amount set aside would include:

If a condition of the original approval requires other levels of approval for encumbering amounts set aside, the levels established in the original contract approval will take precedence

The amount of the set aside should be based on sound front-end planning and preparation:

the better the planning and preparation the better the ability to predict additional quantities or unforeseen work arising, and the more accurate the assigned dollar values

a procurement plan that is thoroughly justified as to the purpose of each planned expenditure, is more likely to be approved

Some examples of the vehicles used to utilize the amount of the set-aside are contract amendment, work authorization form, form DND-626 Requisition on a Contract, or a pre-approved amount for anticipated amendments

Advance approval for options

When you're exercising contract options, follow these specific steps to ensure everything is done correctly:

Get approval for the option: Make sure you have the necessary approval to exercise the contract option. This approval should align with what was outlined in the original approval document. Ensure that all conditions and requirements are met as per the initial contract. Once approval has been obtained to exercise an option, the contract amendment to exercise the option requires only the signature of an incumbent, under any level, who holds a valid delegation of approval authority for contract entry for the same categories for which the incumbent has delegation of authority

Check delegation of approval authority for contract entry : Verify that you have the appropriate delegation of authority to sign the contract amendment. You need to have the authority for the same categories as the contract option. For example, if you have the delegated authority for goods and military repair and overhaul, you can sign for those categories

Separate aggregate values: Treat the aggregate values of previously approved options separately from the aggregate amendment values of either set-asides or normal amendments. You can exercise options up to their maximum value in one amendment if needed

Use funds as approved: Use the funds and approval for the option strictly for the purposes specified in the original approval document. For example, if a one-year services contract is approved for $200,000 with an option year also valued at $200,000, you can't use the option year's funds to cover an increase in services during the first year

Sign the contract amendment: Once you have the necessary approvals and verified your authority, sign the contract amendment. Only someone with the correct delegation of authority for the relevant contract categories should sign

Contract code for pre-approved changes (in SELECT)

Contract Code for Pre-approved Amendments is available in SELECT and is used to identify all pre-approved amendments.

These include, but are not limited to:

Amendments issued to exercise an option for additional quantities or years that formed part of the original bid solicitation and approved as part of the original contract approval submission

Amendments which use amounts set-aside for anticipated, but not yet clearly defined, changes (e.g., unscheduled work, design changes, work arising), which were approved as part of the original contract approval submission

Amendments that do not fit within the description of the original approval or go beyond the scope or value of the approval will be coded as "Normal Amendments" and must be approved at the appropriate level

The appropriate level for procurements within Complexity Levels 1 through 3 inclusive is determined by completing a RAA

For procurements within Complexity Levels 4 and 5 , the approval of the Assistant Deputy Minister – Procurement Branch or Defence and Marine Procurement Branch is required

Rates (or prices-to-be-negotiated)/interim rates (or prices)

If the basis of payment in a contract includes interim rates (or prices), and it is proposed to amend the contract to provide for firm rates (or prices) in lieu, then the amendment approval authority level will be as follows (unless otherwise instructed by the contract approval authority):

Contracting authority: only if the firm rates (or prices) are equal to or less than the interim rates (or prices)

Director general, if the contract was approved by the ADM , the Minister or TB

For Level 1, 2 and 3 procurements, a RAA is required to determine the appropriate approval authority

Combining several types of contract changes

When a contract amendment involves multiple types of changes, the approval level required corresponds to the highest level for any single type of change or combinations of types being incorporated by the amendment.

Amendments to contracts with former public servants

TB must approve all amendments to service contracts with former public servants in receipt of a pension pursuant to the Public Service Superannuation Act when these amendments exceed the limits in the Delegation of authority instrument (accessible only on the Government of Canada network - for PSPC only). See also Approvals and authorities for information on Ministerial required for an amendment to a non-competitive service contract with a former public servant.

Interpretation of aggregate amendment value

Aggregate amendment value means the sum total of the amendments to a contract, including positive and negative amendments. It applies only to amendments requiring TB approval.

For internal purposes and to ensure adequate control measures are in place, the aggregate amount for amendments not pre-approved will be calculated using only positive amendments.

Nil value amendments where no further risk or liability accrued to Canada are administrative in nature and can be approved by the Contracting Authority. For nil value amendments where further risk or liability will be Canada's responsibility, the completion of a RAA is required to determine the appropriate approval authority.

Extending the period of a method of supply (that is, a contract, contract with TA s, SO , or SA ) with a specific end date cannot be treated as administrative in nature. Adding additional time to the contract represents additional liability for Canada regardless of whether or not additional funding is required.

For example, if a contract has been awarded for a two-year period ending March 31 in the amount of $10 million, and there is $4 million left over on April 1st, that $4 million cannot be used for an extension of the contract without obtaining the appropriate approval, even though the original approval to contract was for $10 million.

Extension of the time element of contracts or contractual arrangements approved by Treasury Board

As per TBS Contracting Policy Notice 2000-3 , the time element of TB approved contracts/contractual arrangements may be extended without further TB approval only when all of the following conditions are met:

The extension’s maximum duration is the lesser of two years or 50 percent of the duration of the contract/contractual arrangement (including pre-approved amendments)

There is no financial implication to an end date amendment or the financial impact is within the contracting limits specified in the TB Directive on the Management of Procurement

The end date included in the original TB submission was not considered a critical part of the TB approval

The contract/contractual arrangement period in the original TB submission was indicated to be approximate or non-specific

Note: “No financial implication” does not mean that there can be no spending in the extension period. It means the spending during the time extension must remain within scope and value of the TB approved contract/contractual arrangement

Considering that the end date is contingent on various factors, delivery/time frame in the TB submission is provided as an estimate, using project planning tools, assumptions and a best case scenario. For instance, the precise time frame may vary based on factors such as:

the actual start date

uncontrollable delays that impacted the delivery/time frame

nature and complexity of the work

the work environment

Therefore, this date may not have been a critical piece of the requirement or TB submission and will need to be determined on a case by case basis and explained.

You must carefully assess the circumstances and reasons that warrant extending the time element of a TB approved contract/contractual arrangement. Your assessment must be documented on file and should address the following questions:

Provide supporting justification for the decision to proceed with the contract/contractual arrangement extension

Explain how the extension meets all of the above identified TB conditions for amending the contract/contractual arrangement without returning to TB for approval

For the scope of the contract/contractual arrangement:

confirm that contractual expenditures are still within the scope of the TB approval

confirm that the projected expenditures in the proposed extension are reasonable or supported by historical spend on the contract/contractual arrangement

confirm that the deliverables are the same

confirm that the terms and conditions are not being compromised

confirm whether the contract/contractual arrangement are substantively changing from how it was initially presented, and whether TB ministers would have an interest

outline if the delivery/time frame was a key element of the TB approval

in the case of services contracts/contractual arrangements, discuss what industry expectations were

outline when the period of service was expected to be up for renewal and for tender

confirm whether there new suppliers in the market that would question the fairness of the extension

If the contractor is responsible for the work delay but the extension can be justified (such as insufficient time to recompete or other exceptional circumstances), explain what Vendor Performance Corrective Measures have been taken against the contractor to mitigate the procurement risk for the remainder of the contract/contractual arrangement

Adding additional time to a contract/contractual arrangement (outside of pre-approved amendments for pre-approved option years) represents additional liability for Canada. and therefore, must not be treated as an administrative amendment. You must obtain the appropriate internal approval for the contract/contractual arrangement extension amendment.

The approval authority for the time extension amendment will depend on the complexity and risk levels as follows:

For complexity levels 1, 2 and 3 , the RAA must be used to determine the approval authority

For complexity levels 4 and 5 , the approval authority is the Acquisitions Program ADM - Defence and Marine Procurement Branch or ADM - Procurement Branch

Part 1 Submission Data – Form PWGSC-TPSGC 1151-1 Proposal Additional costs (or reduction in cost) Remarks Authority Part 2 Supporting Data - Form PWGSC-TPSGC 1151-4 Section A - Physical Progress to Date Section B - Authorities for and Status of Contract, plus Amount of Proposed Amendment Section C - Basis and method of payment as last amended Section D - Basis of recommendation

Contract amendment

The contract amendment approval document is used to seek approval to amend a contract, standing offer (SO) or supply arrangement (SA). The amendment approval authority required will determine the document to be used.

Here are some general considerations to keep in mind when preparing a contract amendment:

Evaluate the current state of the contract, including progress and any previous amendments

Outline the details of the proposed amendment

Provide a clear rationale for any changes in the payment basis, methods, or overall contract terms, supported by detailed documentation

Consult Legal Services before considering a conditional amendment

There are four types of contract amendments. The following outlines the four types and the approval authority required for each.

1. Administrative contract amendments

Administrative contract amendments are used for changes that do not create additional risk or liability to Canada and do not increase the contract value (that is, a nil or reduction in contract value amendment) They typically include, changes to update contact information or reduce the contract value for quantities of goods or work no longer required under the contract, with no increase in the unit price.

Since there is no impact on the approval value and no additional risk or liability to Canada they do not require a Risk Assessment for Amendment (RAA).

Approval authority: An administrative contract amendment can be approved and signed by any incumbent, under any level, who holds a valid delegation of approval authority for contract entry for the applicable product and service categories

2. Nil or reduction in contract value amendments involving additional risk to Canada

A contract amendment can involve additional risks to Canada even though the contract value is not changing or is being reduced. This applies to amendments that propose changes to the basis of payment, the method of payment, or the contract conditions, so that additional risk or liability is transferred to Canada.

This type of amendment can be used when a client:

reduces the quantity required but the contractor wants to increase the price because of the reduced quantity, triggering a need for renegotiation

requires a work package to be removed from a research and development contract, but there is no clear relationship between the reduced cost and the reduced work package

requests you to amend a contract by deleting or substituting an item or work package

Nil or reduction in contract value amendments involving additional risk to Canada require a Risk Assessment for Amendment (RAA) to assess and manage the additional risks involved and must be authorized in accordance with the approval level determined by completing the RAA .

3. Pre-approved contract amendments

Pre-approved contract amendments exercise options or authorize work that was previously identified and approved in the original approval documentation. Pre-approved contract amendments include:

amendments to exercise an option for additional quantities or years initially included in the original solicitation and approved in the original contract approval submission

amendments which use amounts set-aside for anticipated but not yet clearly defined work, or changes, such as unscheduled work, design changes, or work arising, that were approved as part of the original contract submission

This amendment is pre-approved, as the work and funding were identified and approved in the original contract approval documentation. In accordance with departmental guidance, an RAA was completed, with the ''value'' question assessed using the approved set-aside amount. No additional approval beyond the delegated authority identified through the RAA is required.

4. Unforeseen contract amendments not pre-approved

Sometimes contract amendments are required to incorporate work or contract changes that were neither foreseen nor pre-approved in the original contract approval. Unforeseen contract amendments not approved can include:

purchasing additional quantities not previously anticipated and not pre-approved

adding an additional requirement to the contract not previously anticipated and not pre-approved

extending the contract period due to unexpected delays (beyond the contractor’s control) to allow all work under the contract to be completed

increasing the contract value beyond the original approval

Approval for this type of amendment must be secured at the appropriate level:

For amendments within complexity levels 1 through 3 inclusive , the appropriate level required is determined by completing a RAA

For amendments that fall within complexity levels 4 and 5 , the approval of the Public Services and Procurement Canada (PSPC) Assistant Deputy Minister (ADM), Procurement Branch, or Defence and Marine Procurement Branch, is required

Preparing and securing approval for contract amendments

When preparing and securing approval for contract amendments, it's essential to consider the nature and value of the proposed changes. This process ensures that all amendments align with organizational policies and receive the necessary approvals.

Here's how to determine the required approval for a contract amendment:

Before proceeding with a contract amendment, complete the RAA to identify the risks associated with the proposed changes and determine the appropriate approval authority. Note: that administrative amendments and pre-approved options are exceptions to this step.

For proposed contract amendments that have not been previously approved and fall within complexity levels 1 through 3, you must complete the RAA to identify the suitable approval authority.

When responding to the "value" question in the RAA , consider the total value of the proposed amendment and the cumulative value of all positive amendments (previous and proposed). Therefore, use the aggregate amendment value (both past and proposed) when determining the dollar amount. Note : This excludes pre-approved amendments identified and approved in the original approval documents

Unless specified within the notes to contracting limits including exceptional authorities outlined in the PSPC Delegation of authorities instrument (accessible only on the Government of Canada network - for PSPC only), exceptional authorities include both contract entry and amendment authority. Consequently, the dollar value considered must include the original contract value and the aggregate amendment value (both past and proposed).

Examples for when a RAA is required for a complexity level 1 through 3 contract awarded on an electronically competed basis can be found on the Determining the Amendment Approval Authority (accessible only on the Government of Canada network).

Contract amendments requiring Director General or Regional Director General approval and below

You may use form 1151 - Contract Amendment Request (CAR) .

The form is to ensure that all necessary documentation is systematically presented for assessment and approval.

If a RAA is required for the proposed amendment, it must be attached and the results discussed in the contract amendment request.

You must include the following information in the contract amendment request:

a concise description of the goods or services as provided in the original Contract Planning and Advance Approval (CPAA), followed by a clear statement of the proposed amendment’s purpose

total cost of the proposed amendment (increase/decrease) in Canadian dollars or foreign currency, as applicable (if using foreign currency, give the equivalent in Canadian currency for the amendment only)

name of the client and any other relevant information

a table summarizing the approval documents, the document value, the approval value of each approval document, and the approval level

the proposed basis of payment, and justification and support for any proposed changes to the basis of payment

a list of the identified risks, particularly those classified as medium-high to high, and describe the proposed risk response measures for mitigation

provide the following information, as a minimum:

a justification for the amendment

any additional information required for a proper assessment of the proposed amendment (for example, if the amendment is for a substantial cost increase, explain why this additional requirement did not form part of the original requirement)

Amendments requiring approval of Assistant Deputy Minister level and above

Present a comprehensive proposal that includes the amendment's purpose, detailed description of changes, cost implications, and any deviations from standard policies. Outline the methodology for additional costs, including a detailed breakdown and justification for any increases or reductions in the contract value.

You must use the two-part contract amendment request form and present it in either French or English:

PWGSC-TPSGC 1151-1: Contract Request/Contract Amendment Request (Part 1 - Submission Data) (accessible only on the Government of Canada network - for PSPC only), and

PWGSC-TPSGC 1151-4: Contract Amendment Request (Part 2 - Supporting Data) (accessible only on the Government of Canada network - for PSPC only)

Authority to amend contract or authority to revise a standing offer method of supply. Form PWGSC-TPSGC 1151-1 can be used to obtain approval to utilize a standing offer that has been revised by the offeror.

The key elements you should include in a contract amendment request are the purpose of the amendment and the amendment cost. Do not show amounts in Part 1.

The following information provides additional considerations for part 1 of the form, when applicable.

State the purpose of the proposed amendment and briefly describe the goods and/or services, as provided in the original contract request (for example, to amend the contract with ABC for the supply of 20 additional widgets). Include, in the case of goods or services being added, the prices, sales tax position, delivery points, etc.

Identify any differences between funds previously authorized and contract commitments.

If a large number of items are involved, state:

"Unit (and/or lot) prices, totalling $ [insert amount], sales tax [insert sales tax], and FOB [insert FOB], as detailed in the attached annex or in an annex attached to the proposed contract."

If the proposed amendment involves any deviations from Cabinet or TB contracting policies, not included in the original approval, describe the deviations fully.

Show the total cost of the proposed amendment in Canadian dollars or foreign currency, as applicable (if using foreign currency, give the equivalent in Canadian currency).

Show the proposed amended estimated cost of the contract . If the previously authorized total contains an amount for specific future work or foreseen yet unscheduled work (such as design changes or work arising), always include this amount in the total estimated cost. If not, authority for the amount set aside is lost.

Provide the cash flow and other information, as explained under the "Cost" section of the contract request.

Provide a brief description of previous amendments and their cost.

Give additional important information required for a proper assessment of the proposed amendment. For example, if the proposed amendment is for a substantial increase, state why this additional requirement did not form part of the original requirement. Refer to the Remarks section of the Contract Request for a guide to the information which should be provided, if applicable.

When a contract amendment request requires a higher approval level than originally authorized in the contract, detail the basis for the selection of the contractor and the basis of payment. It is not necessary to repeat in Part 1, the present basis of payment if it was previously approved at a higher level or by the contracting authority being approached now for approval of this amendment.

If any alteration in the basis of payment is proposed, provide justification and support. If a difference exists between funds authorized and contractual commitments, explain why.

Provide details surrounding the medium-high and high risks relating to the proposed amendment with proposed mitigation strategies.

If items are being added, or when establishing a firm basis of payment for a contract, previously issued on a price-to-be-negotiated (PTBN) basis, provide price support.

Show the original authority for entry into the contract and the authority for each approved amendment.

When TB authority has been obtained, give the TB number and date. When the Minister's approval has been obtained, state "Ministerial authority." In all other cases, state "departmental authority."

Provide all supporting information, using the following guidance for completion of part 2 of the form.

Summarize the progress of the contract, such as quantities already delivered and the percentage completed; work in progress or completed; advance or progress payments made or any other preliminary expenses; and other matters of a similar nature.

Give specific authorities and authorized amounts under the "Authority and Amount" column for the contract and each amendment (for example, Treasury Board, Minister, Deputy Minister, Director General, Director).

Any differences between authorities (approvals) and commitments should be reconciled in Section B.

If the amount of the proposed amendment exceeds the funds available, it should be noted in this section.

Describe briefly the basis of payment and method of payment as last amended, including sales tax position, delivery terms (for example, FOB ), advance and progress payments).

However, if it is proposed to amend the basis of payment or method of payment, give a detailed description of the present basis of payment and method of payment for any portion of the work for which a new basis of payment or method of payment is being recommended.

Support price and changes in terms or method of payment.

Detail all price support for any items being added or when establishing a firm basis of payment for a contract, previously issued on a PTBN basis.

Explain any discrepancies between the amount approved for the contract and amendments (if any), and the total committed.

Note: If numerous items and unit prices have not been detailed in part 1 of form PWGSC-TPSGC 1151-1 (accessible only on the government of Canada network - for PSPC only), or in an annex to Part 1, make reference in Section D to the specific document of the file that details the information.

Contract amendments requiring Treasury Board approval

For contract amendments that fall under TB authority, a detailed submission is required.

You must use the TB submission form and include:

a clear demonstration of the need for the amendment, its impact on the contract value, and how it adheres to or diverges from TB 's guidelines

consideration of cumulative values of all contract amendments to assess the overall impact on the contract and adherence to exceptional authorities as applicable

Extension of the time element of contracts or contractual arrangements approved by Treasury Board

As per TBS Contracting Policy Notice 2000-3 , the time element of TB approved contracts/contractual arrangements may be extended without further TB approval only when all of the following conditions are met:

The extension’s maximum duration is the lesser of two years or 50 percent of the duration of the contract/contractual arrangement (including pre-approved amendments)

There is no financial implication to an end date amendment or the financial impact is within the contracting limits specified in the TB Directive on the Management of Procurement

The end date included in the original TB submission was not considered a critical part of the TB approval

The contract/contractual arrangement period in the original TB submission was indicated to be approximate or non-specific

Note: “No financial implication” does not mean that there can be no spending in the extension period. It means the spending during the time extension must remain within scope and value of the TB approved contract/contractual arrangement

Considering that the end date is contingent on various factors, delivery/time frame in the TB submission is provided as an estimate, using project planning tools, assumptions and a best case scenario. For instance, the precise time frame may vary based on factors such as:

the actual start date

uncontrollable delays that impacted the delivery/time frame

nature and complexity of the work

the work environment

Therefore, this date may not have been a critical piece of the requirement or TB submission and will need to be determined on a case by case basis and explained.

You must carefully assess the circumstances and reasons that warrant extending the time element of a TB approved contract/contractual arrangement. Your assessment must be documented on file and should address the following questions:

Provide supporting justification for the decision to proceed with the contract/contractual arrangement extension

Explain how the extension meets all of the above identified TB conditions for amending the contract/contractual arrangement without returning to TB for approval

For the scope of the contract/contractual arrangement:

confirm that contractual expenditures are still within the scope of the TB approval

confirm that the projected expenditures in the proposed extension are reasonable or supported by historical spend on the contract/contractual arrangement

confirm that the deliverables are the same

confirm that the terms and conditions are not being compromised

confirm whether the contract/contractual arrangement are substantively changing from how it was initially presented, and whether TB ministers would have an interest

outline if the delivery/time frame was a key element of the TB approval

in the case of services contracts/contractual arrangements, discuss what industry expectations were

outline when the period of service was expected to be up for renewal and for tender

confirm whether there new suppliers in the market that would question the fairness of the extension

If the contractor is responsible for the work delay but the extension can be justified (such as insufficient time to recompete or other exceptional circumstances), explain what Vendor Performance Corrective Measures have been taken against the contractor to mitigate the procurement risk for the remainder of the contract/contractual arrangement

Adding additional time to a contract/contractual arrangement (outside of pre-approved amendments for pre-approved option years) represents additional liability for Canada. and therefore, must not be treated as an administrative amendment. You must obtain the appropriate internal approval for the contract/contractual arrangement extension amendment.

The approval authority for the time extension amendment will depend on the complexity and risk levels as follows:

For complexity levels 1, 2 and 3 , the RAA must be used to determine the approval authority

For complexity levels 4 and 5 , the approval authority is the Acquisitions Program ADM - Defence and Marine Procurement Branch or ADM - Procurement Branch
